title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill would give a special Congressional Gold Medal to wildland firefighters as a group. The medal would honor their brave work fighting fires to protect America's forests, grasslands, and communities. The bill recognizes how dangerous their job is and how they sacrifice to keep people safe. The Treasury Department would also be allowed to make bronze copies of the medal to sell to the public.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S231-232)

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned the following facts. Wildfires have gotten more dangerous and severe over the past thirty years. These fires have destroyed homes, roads, bridges, and valuable government, state, and private lands. More than one billion acres of land across the United States could catch fire. This includes about one hundred seventeen million acres of federal land that experts say has a high or very high chance of wildfire. Five government agencies work together to fight wildfires. The Forest Service, the Bureau of Land Management, the National Park Service, the United States Fish and Wildlife Service, and the Bureau of Indian Affairs employ more than eighteen thousand seven hundred wildland firefighters. These firefighters fight wildfires across millions of acres of public and private lands each year. Thousands more workers and volunteers serve as state, local, and contract wildland firefighters. As cities and towns spread closer to wild areas, wildfires are moving out of remote areas and into neighborhoods across the United States. Wildland firefighters are learning new skills and methods to fight the growing wildfire threat in the twenty first century. While protecting people and property is most important, wildland firefighters also help manage forests responsibly. Wildland firefighters do controlled burns and other forest management work like timber harvests. This helps keep forests healthy and reduces the risk of dangerous wildfires. Each wildland firefighter gets special training to work in dangerous and constantly changing conditions. Wildland firefighters often work long days during two week work periods and sleep in harsh conditions. According to the Forest Service, firefighters usually work sixteen hour days while fighting a fire. They typically work more than two thousand five hundred hours during a six month period. Wildland firefighter crews respond to all kinds of emergencies on the front lines. They use whatever methods are needed to protect people and property when responding to floods, hurricanes, disease outbreaks, and terrorist attacks. Engine crews and hand crews are the main firefighting workers. These crews come in different sizes and can be changed to fit the specific needs and land challenges that each fire presents. Interagency hotshot crews are highly skilled mobile hand crews with expert knowledge about how to put out fires. Pilots and aerial fire suppression crews fly air tankers and helicopters to drop water and fire retardant. They support decision makers on the ground. Firefighters delivered by air include helitack crews and smokejumpers. These firefighters get out of helicopters and jump from planes into remote and hard to reach areas. They provide quick and targeted fire suppression and emergency medical evacuation by helicopter. These fire personnel provide oversight and direct action on initial and extended attack incidents. Wildland firefighters in the United States also help fight wildfires in other countries. During the record setting fires in Australia in two thousand twenty, the United States sent three hundred sixty two firefighters to help. During Canada's historic two thousand twenty three fire season, more than two thousand federal wildland firefighters answered the call. As of when this law is passed, the United States has agreements to help with wildland firefighting efforts with Canada, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand, and Portugal. The increase in how severe wildfires are and in how many months of active fire season there are has also increased the need for wildland firefighters and related workers. Finding and keeping wildland firefighters has been a national problem for many years. Wildland firefighters risk their lives to keep the people of the United States safe. Some pay the ultimate sacrifice to do so. Between January first two thousand nineteen and January first two thousand twenty five, ninety eight wildland firefighters have lost their lives fighting fires. Direct and secondary effects from wildfire such as wildfire smoke exposure are directly linked to tens of thousands of firefighter and civilian deaths each year. June thirtieth to July sixth of each year is recognized as a Week of Remembrance to honor the fallen wildland firefighters who sacrificed their lives to protect the wildlands of the United States. National Wildland Firefighter Day is held each year on July second to recognize all who are devoted to wildland firefighting. The outstanding efforts of wildland firefighters deserve recognition. It is right and proper to honor those who have served before as well as current and future firefighters. Wildland firefighters show principles of duty, respect, and integrity in every aspect of service. Each firefighter shows strength, toughness, and determination to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. Wildland firefighters do not avoid dangerous situations but instead risk life and limb to help others. The outstanding accomplishments of these brave individuals continue an unmatched legacy of public service.

sections.title: Section 3  Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the temporary president of the Senate) shall make proper arrangements for giving one gold medal on behalf of Congress. This medal will have an appropriate design to honor wildland firefighters as a group. This recognizes their strength, toughness, sacrifice, and service to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury (called the Secretary in this law) shall make a gold medal with suitable symbols, pictures, and words. The Secretary will decide what these should be after talking with the National Interagency Fire Center (the main coordination center for wildland firefighting). After the gold medal is given, it shall go to the National Interagency Fire Center. The gold medal shall be displayed properly and made available for research. It is Congress's opinion that the National Interagency Fire Center should make sure the medal is displayed and available at proper locations, especially locations connected with wildland firefighters.

sections.title: Section 4  Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell copies in bronze of the gold medal made under section three. The price must be enough to cover all costs including labor, materials, dies (the molds used to make medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5  Status of Medals
sections.text: The medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one United States Code (the part of federal law that covers national medals). For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one United States Code, all medals made under this law shall be considered to be numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6  Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: Money can be taken from the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from selling duplicate bronze medals allowed under section four shall be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
